```mermaid
    MUSC[MUSCLE TENDON FORCE GENERATORS] --> JNT
    JNT --> KNM[KNEE NET MOMENT]
    JNT --> HPM[HIP NET MOMENT]
    JNT --> ANM[ANKLE NET MOMENT]
    KNM --> PROC[PROSTHETIC SUBSTITUTION BLOCK]
    HPM --> PROC
    ANM --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT1[ENERGY STORAGE FOOT]
    PROC --> OUT2[MPK STANCE CONTROL]
    PROC --> OUT3[POLYCENTRIC KNEE]
    SAU[SAUNDERS DETERMINANTS 1 TO 6] --> OUT1
    SAU --> OUT2
    SAU --> OUT3
```

**Block Functional Roles:**

| Block | Function in the Gait Model |
| :--- | :--- |
| Ground Reaction Force Vector | Input kinetic load from force plate (3 components $F_x, F_y, F_z$). |
| Body Segment Inertia | Newtonian $F = m \cdot a$ inputs from each limb segment. |
| Muscle-Tendon Force Generators | Hill-type actuator models providing active joint torque. |
| Joint Moment Computation | Inverse dynamics: $M = I\alpha + \text{reaction terms}$. |
| Prosthetic Substitution Block | Replaces lost muscular torque for amputee subjects. |
| Saunders Determinants | Provides kinematic target trajectory to mimic. |

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Three Foot Rocker Mechanisms (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1["HEEL ROCKER\n0 to 10 percent GC\nShock absorption\nHeel compresses, tibia advances"]
    R2["ANKLE ROCKER\n10 to 30 percent GC\nControlled dorsiflexion\nTibia rolls over talus"]
    R3["FOREFOOT ROCKER\n30 to 50 percent GC\nPush off\nMetatarsals pivot"]
    R1 --> R2 --> R3
    R1 -.-> PR1[PROSTHETIC REQUIREMENT\nCompliant heel keel]
    R2 -.-> PR2[PROSTHETIC REQUIREMENT\nAnkle dorsiflexion dampening]
    R3 -.-> PR3[PROSTHETIC REQUIREMENT\nEnergy returning forefoot]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Connection:** When asked *"How does a SACH foot differ from a Carbon-Fiber Energy-Storing foot?"*, the answer is: *SACH provides only the heel rocker; carbon-fiber feet provide all three rockers, with energy return at the forefoot rocker.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Gait Cycle. List the two major phases and state the percentage of the cycle occupied by each in normal adult walking.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Gait Cycle** is defined as the interval of time between two successive occurrences of the same event of the same limb — typically from one **Heel Strike (HS)** to the next Heel Strike of the same foot.

The two major phases are:

- **Stance Phase:** The reference foot is in contact with the ground → occupies **≈ 60%** of the gait cycle.
- **Swing Phase:** The reference foot is off the ground → occupies **≈ 40%** of the gait cycle.

Additionally, there are two short **Double Limb Support (DLS)** intervals, each ≈ 10% of GC, occurring at Loading Response and Pre-Swing.

*(Word count: 60 words — fits 3-mark answer.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the "Normalised Walking Speed" (NWS) and why is it clinically important in prosthetic gait prescription?*

**Model Answer:**

NWS is a dimensionless number that scales walking speed to a subject's leg length, given by:
$$
NWS = \frac{v}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{leg}}}
$$
For a typical adult, $NWS \approx 0.47$ at free walking speed, and $NWS \approx 0.5$ marks the **walk-run transition**.

**Clinical importance:**
1. Allows comparison of gait across subjects of different heights.
2. Prosthetic knee/foot components (e.g., C-Leg, Genium, Blatchford ECHELON) are tuned for a specific NWS window; the prescription must match the amputee's habitual NWS.
3. It is used as the **Froude-equivalent scaling parameter** in prosthetic bench testing.

*(3 marks: definition 1 mark, formula 1 mark, clinical use 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Describe the **Rancho Los Amigos (RLA) 8-Phase classification** of the gait cycle with a neatly labelled phase diagram and the approximate percentage of cycle for each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using inverse dynamics, calculate the **peak external knee flexion moment** during Loading Response for a 70 kg subject. Given: vertical GRF at 10% GC = **0.6 × BW**, horizontal GRF = **0.15 × BW**, perpendicular distance from knee joint center to GRF line of action in sagittal plane = **0.08 m**. Hence determine the **quadriceps tendon force** required. Patellar tendon moment arm = 0.05 m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q3A

**(a) RLA 8-Phase Classification (7 marks)**

| Phase # | Name | % GC | Stance/Swing | Key Event |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Initial Contact (IC) | 0 – 2 | Stance | Heel touches ground |
| 2 | Loading Response (LR) | 2 – 12 | Stance | Foot flat; DLS #1 |
| 3 | Mid-Stance (MSt) | 12 – 31 | Stance | Trunk over foot |
| 4 | Terminal Stance (TSt) | 31 – 50 | Stance | Heel rise; SLS |
| 5 | Pre-Swing (PSw) | 50 – 62 | Stance | Toe-off; DLS #2 |
| 6 | Initial Swing (ISw) | 62 – 75 | Swing | Knee flexes |
| 7 | Mid-Swing (MSw) | 75 – 87 | Swing | Tibia vertical |
| 8 | Terminal Swing (TSw) | 87 – 100 | Swing | Knee extends, decelerates |

**Diagram:** (Refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4 — full Mermaid flowchart).

**Key Annotations:**
- Stance occupies 60% (Phases 1–5), Swing occupies 40% (Phases 6–8).
- Two DLS intervals: 0–12% and 50–62%.
- Single Limb Support = 31% – 50% = 19% (one per limb), summing to 38%, with the rest of stance covered by DLS.

**(b) Knee Moment & Quadriceps Force Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert body weight to Newtons:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= m \cdot g = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7\ \text{N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute vertical GRF at Loading Response:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_z &= 0.6 \times BW = 0.6 \times 686.7 = 412.02\ \text{N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute external flexion moment (sagittal plane, vertical component dominates):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{knee} &= F_z \times d = 412.02 \times 0.08 \\
M_{knee} &= 32.96\ \text{Nm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the (small) anterior-posterior contribution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_y &= 0.15 \times 686.7 = 103.0\ \text{N} \\
M_{knee,total} &\approx 32.96\ \text{Nm (vertical dominates; AP adds <5% so rounding is acceptable)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for quadriceps tendon force using static equilibrium:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{quad} \times d_{lever} &= M_{knee} \\
F_{quad} &= \frac{32.96}{0.05} \\
F_{quad} &= 659.2\ \text{N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The quadriceps must produce ≈ **659 N** of tendon force at Loading Response — almost equal to the entire body weight. This explains why knee osteoarthritis and amputee gait are so debilitating and why **stance-control prosthetic knees** are essential.

---

**Q3B. (Alternative for Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain **Saunders' Six Determinants of Gait** and their role in minimising vertical displacement of the body's center of mass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A subject walks with stride length $L_{stride} = 1.4$ m at a cadence of 110 steps/min. Calculate (i) the walking speed, (ii) the gait cycle time, and (iii) the Normalised Walking Speed (NWS) assuming leg length = 0.9 m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q3B

**(a) Saunders' Six Determinants (7 marks)**

The six determinants, originally proposed by **Saunders, Inman, and Eberhart (1953)**, are kinematic strategies that minimise the **8th-order sinusoidal vertical COM displacement** to a smoother 4th-order curve of amplitude ≈ **5 cm** (vs. ≈ 9 cm if the leg were a rigid stick).

| # | Determinant | Magnitude | Effect on COM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pelvic Rotation (horizontal) | 4° each side | Smooths arc length |
| 2 | Pelvic Tilt (coronal) | 5° on swing side | Lowers COM at mid-stance |
| 3 | Stance-Knee Flexion | 15° at LR | Lowers peak; absorbs shock |
| 4 | Foot Rockers (3 rockers) | Heel/Ankle/Forefoot | Rounds the trajectory |
| 5 | Knee Interaction | Coupled with #1 | Reduces vertical displacement |
| 6 | Lateral Pelvic Displacement | 2.5 cm | Shifts COM over stance foot |

**Composite Effect (mathematically):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta z_{COM,\ rigid\ stick} &\approx 8.9\ \text{cm (peak-to-peak)} \\
\Delta z_{COM,\ 6\ determinants} &\approx 4.5\ \text{cm (peak-to-peak)} \\
\%\ reduction &= \frac{8.9 - 4.5}{8.9} \times 100 \approx 49.4\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Application:** Modern **prosthetic feet with multi-axial ankles** and **microprocessor knees** reproduce determinants #3, #4, and #5 in particular, thereby reducing the amputee's compensatory trunk motion and energy cost.

**(b) Gait Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L_{stride} = 1.4$ m
- $f_c = 110$ steps/min
- $L_{leg} = 0.9$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Walking speed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{L_{stride} \times f_c}{120} \\
v &= \frac{1.4 \times 110}{120} \\
v &= \frac{154}{120} \\
v &= 1.283\ \text{m/s} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Gait cycle time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{GC} &= \frac{60}{f_c} \times 2 = \frac{120}{110} \\
T_{GC} &\approx 1.091\ \text{s} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Normalised Walking Speed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
NWS &= \frac{v}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{leg}}} \\
NWS &= \frac{1.283}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.9}} \\
NWS &= \frac{1.283}{\sqrt{8.829}} \\
NWS &= \frac{1.283}{2.971} \\
NWS &\approx 0.432 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $NWS = 0.432 < 0.5$ → subject is in the **walking regime** (not yet at walk-run transition). This NWS value helps select the appropriate **energy-storing carbon-fiber prosthetic foot category** (low to medium impact).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide cadence by 2 when using stride length.** Cadence is in *steps* per minute, not strides. The 120 in the denominator is mandatory.
> 2. **Confusing step length with stride length.** Stride = 2 × step. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Writing "stance = 50%, swing = 50%".** This is for *running*, not walking. Walking is 60/40.
> 4. **Confusing Initial Contact with Heel Strike timing.** Heel Strike is at 0%; Loading Response extends to 12%.
> 5. **Forgetting units in inverse-dynamics solutions.** Mark loss of 0.5 – 1 per question.
> 6. **Not converting body weight to Newtons** in the GRF/moment calculation.
> 7. **Quoting cadence in Hz instead of steps/min** — KTU expects steps/min.
> 8. **Ignoring Double Limb Support** when discussing running-vs-walking distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Gait Cycle** = HS to HS of same foot; **60% Stance, 40% Swing**.
- [ ] **Step length = 0.7 m, Stride length = 1.4 m, Cadence = 100–120 steps/min, Speed = 1.4 m/s** (mugging values).
- [ ] **RLA 8 Phases:** IC → LR → MSt → TSt → PSw → ISw → MSw → TSw.
- [ ] **Two DLS intervals** = 0–12% and 50–62% of GC. DLS = 0% in running.
- [ ] **Saunders' 6 Determinants** reduce vertical COM excursion from 8.9 cm → 4.5 cm.
- [ ] **3 Foot Rockers:** Heel (0–10%), Ankle (10–30%), Forefoot (30–50%).
- [ ] **Vertical GRF peak ≈ 1.2 × BW** at Terminal Stance; AP GRF ≈ **0.2 × BW**.
- [ ] **Knee flexion peaks:** 15° at Loading Response, 40° at Mid-Swing.
- [ ] **Hip ROM total ≈ 40–50°, Knee ≈ 60–70°, Ankle ≈ 30°** (sagittal plane).
- [ ] **Step Width ≈ 8–10 cm; Toe-out angle ≈ 7°**.
- [ ] **Velocity formula:** $v = (L_{stride} \times f_c)/120$ — *the* most-tested equation.
- [ ] **NWS ≈ 0.5 → walk-run transition threshold.**
- [ ] **Prosthetic design link:** SACH foot = heel rocker only; Carbon-fiber = all three rockers + energy return.
- [ ] **Inverse-dynamics knee moment** during LR ≈ **0.6 × BW × 0.08 m ≈ 33 Nm** for a 70 kg subject.
- [ ] **Quadriceps tendon force** during LR ≈ 1 × BW (≈ 660 N) → explains transfemoral amputee stance-phase instability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT601 - BIOMECHANICS & DESIGN OF PROSTHETIC DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of biomechanics and prosthetic device design, the term **"Phases"** refers to the **sequential, time-bound subdivisions of the human gait cycle (locomotion cycle)**. The gait cycle is defined as the time interval between two successive occurrences of the same repetitive event during walking — most commonly measured from one **heel strike (initial contact)** of one foot to the next heel strike of the *same* foot.

The complete gait cycle is divided into two principal phases:

1. **Stance Phase** — the period when the foot is in contact with the ground.
2. **Swing Phase** — the period when the foot is off the ground and moving forward.

The Stance Phase is further subdivided into **five sub-phases (events)**: Heel Strike (Initial Contact), Foot Flat (Loading Response), Mid-Stance, Heel Off (Terminal Stance), and Toe Off (Pre-Swing). The Swing Phase is subdivided into three sub-phases: Acceleration (Initial Swing), Mid-Swing, and Deceleration (Terminal Swing). Additionally, the gait cycle contains **two double-support periods** (when both feet are on the ground) and **one single-support period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For a normal adult walking at a comfortable cadence of ~1.4 m/s, the Stance Phase occupies approximately **60%** of the gait cycle, and the Swing Phase occupies the remaining **40%**. These percentages are NOT fixed — they vary with walking speed, age, pathology, and prosthetic alignment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of human walking as a **pendulum clock mechanism** that you watch from the side. Imagine you freeze the motion and look at one leg — the right leg, for example. The right leg spends some time pressed against the ground (pushing you forward) and some time lifted in the air (swinging ahead like a pendulum to take the next step). 

- The **ground-pressed portion** is the **Stance Phase** — analogous to a "pusher rocket" that propels the body forward.
- The **airborne portion** is the **Swing Phase** — analogous to a "pendulum reset" that repositions the leg for the next step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** When you watch a slow-motion video of a soldier marching, the moment the boot *hits* the ground is Heel Strike; when the entire sole is flat on the ground, it's Foot Flat; the moment the heel starts rising while the toe is still planted is Heel Off; the moment the toe leaves the ground is Toe Off (start of swing). This timing sequence is exactly what a prosthetic limb must replicate to allow an amputee to walk naturally.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Normal Cadence:** **110–120 steps/min** (approximately 2 steps per second)
- **Stride Length (normal):** **1.4–1.8 m** (distance covered in one complete cycle)
- **Step Length (normal):** **0.7–0.9 m** (distance between successive heel strikes of opposite feet)
- **Walking Speed (normal adult):** **1.3–1.5 m/s**
- **Stance Phase Duration:** **~60%** of gait cycle
- **Swing Phase Duration:** **~40%** of gait cycle
- **Double Support:** **~10% each** (two periods, totaling ~20%)
- **Single Support:** **~40%** of gait cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU board exams, always express percentages of the gait cycle, not absolute time. Time changes with cadence, but percentages are normalized and preferred in clinical/biomechanical reporting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Gait Cycle — Structural Breakdown

The gait cycle is conventionally described using **eight functional events/phases** defined by **Jacquelin Perry** (the foundational classification used in clinical gait analysis and prosthetic prescription worldwide, and adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus).

#### 2.1.1 Stance Phase (60% of Gait Cycle)

The stance phase is the weight-bearing portion of gait. It is divided into **five sub-phases**:

**1. Heel Strike / Initial Contact (0% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** The instant the heel of the leading (reference) foot makes contact with the ground.
- **Biomechanics:** The ankle is in **neutral position (0°)**, knee is fully extended (~0°), hip is at ~20–30° flexion.
- **Ground Reaction Force (GRF):** Starts at zero, rapidly rises.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** The prosthetic foot must simulate the heel-strike transient (a small "kink" or rocker) to allow smooth roll-over.

**2. Foot Flat / Loading Response (0–12% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** The period from initial contact until the entire sole of the foot contacts the ground.
- **Biomechanics:** Controlled plantarflexion of the ankle (~5–10°), knee flexes to ~15–20° (shock absorption), hip stabilizes.
- **Muscle activity:** Quadriceps eccentrically contract to control knee flexion; tibialis anterior eccentrically controls plantarflexion.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** Energy-storing prosthetic feet (e.g., carbon-fiber feet) must store and release energy in this phase.

**3. Mid-Stance (12–31% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** Begins when the contralateral foot is lifted (end of opposite stance) and continues until the body weight is aligned over the forefoot of the reference limb.
- **Biomechanics:** Ankle dorsiflexes to ~5°, the tibia advances over the stationary foot, knee reaches near full extension.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** The prosthetic pylon must align to keep the GRF vector passing anterior to the knee for stability in unilateral amputees.

**4. Heel Off / Terminal Stance (31–50% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** The instant the heel of the reference foot leaves the ground; body weight is on the forefoot.
- **Biomechanics:** Ankle plantarflexes actively (~15°), knee extends fully, hip extends to ~10–15° behind vertical.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** The forefoot lever (toe lever) of the prosthetic foot must be rigid enough to provide push-off.

**5. Toe Off / Pre-Swing (50–62% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** The instant the toe of the reference foot leaves the ground; marks the end of stance and the beginning of swing.
- **Biomechanics:** Ankle rapidly plantarflexes (~20°), knee begins to flex (~35–40°), hip flexes to begin limb advancement.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** The transition from stance to swing must be smooth to avoid a "drop-off" effect.

#### 2.1.2 Swing Phase (40% of Gait Cycle)

**6. Acceleration / Initial Swing (62–75% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** The period immediately after toe-off; the limb is accelerated forward.
- **Biomechanics:** Hip flexes actively (iliopsoas), knee flexes to ~60°, ankle dorsiflexes to neutral.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** Lightweight prosthetic components and proper socket suspension are critical to allow easy limb acceleration.

**7. Mid-Swing (75–87% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** The swing limb passes directly beneath the body (the reference limb is directly under the body's center of mass).
- **Biomechanics:** Knee reaches maximum flexion (~60–70°), ankle in neutral dorsiflexion.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** Adequate **swing-phase toe clearance** (minimum 1–2 cm) must be ensured to prevent stumbling.

**8. Deceleration / Terminal Swing (87–100% of cycle)**
- **Definition:** The swing limb decelerates in preparation for heel strike.
- **Biomechanics:** Knee extends toward full extension, ankle remains neutral, hip flexion ~25–30°.
- **Prosthetic relevance:** Hamstrings contract eccentrically to decelerate the limb; prosthetic knee units must have appropriate dampening.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

- **Why divide gait into phases?** — To localize biomechanical events, identify pathological deviations, and design prosthetic components that address specific functional deficits at each phase.
- **Why is the Stance Phase longer (60%) than Swing (40%)?** — Because the body needs more time in weight-bearing for stability and forward propulsion; swing is primarily a "repositioning" task.
- **Why are there double-support periods?** — At slow-normal walking speeds, both feet contact the ground simultaneously at the start and end of each stance phase, providing two periods of maximum stability. **At running speeds (>2.5 m/s), double support disappears and is replaced by a "double float" phase.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are normalized percentages of the gait cycle. The 100% point equals one full cycle (heel strike to next heel strike of the same foot).**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units / Normal Range | Engineering / Prosthetic Utility |
|---|-----------|----------------|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | Stance Phase Duration | $T_{stance} \approx 0.60 \times T_{cycle}$ | % of cycle: **60%** | Determines weight-bearing time of prosthetic limb |
| 2 | Swing Phase Duration | $T_{swing} \approx 0.40 \times T_{cycle}$ | % of cycle: **40%** | Determines non-weight-bearing time of prosthetic limb |
| 3 | Double Support (each) | $T_{DS} \approx 0.10 \times T_{cycle}$ | % of cycle: **10% × 2** | Critical for balance assessment in bilateral amputees |
| 4 | Single Support | $T_{SS} \approx 0.40 \times T_{cycle}$ | % of cycle: **40%** | Time the contralateral limb bears full body weight |
| 5 | Cadence | $C = \frac{2}{T_{cycle}}$ | **110–120 steps/min** | Used to size prosthetic components and gait training |
| 6 | Stride Length | $L_{stride} = v \times T_{cycle}$ | **1.4–1.8 m** | Sets the spatial reference for prosthetic alignment |
| 7 | Step Length | $L_{step} = \frac{L_{stride}}{2}$ | **0.7–0.9 m** | Used to assess symmetry in amputee gait |
| 8 | Walking Speed | $v = L_{stride} \times C$ | **1.3–1.5 m/s** | Determines prosthetic foot energy return category |
| 9 | Dimensionless Speed | $v^{*} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{leg}}}$ | $\approx 0.4$ (normal) | Used in Froude number scaling for prosthetic design |
| 10 | Duty Factor | $DF = \frac{T_{stance}}{T_{cycle}}$ | **0.60** (normal walk), <0.5 (run) | Distinguishes walking from running gait |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **step length** (one foot to the other foot) with **stride length** (same foot to same foot). Stride = 2 × Step. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetic Foot Design:** Energy-storing-and-returning (ESAR) feet mimic the heel-to-toe rocker mechanism of biological gait.
- **Prosthetic Knee Units:** Stance-phase control (weight-activated braking) vs. swing-phase control (pneumatic/hydraulic damping) — each addresses a specific phase.
- **Gait Trainers & Rehabilitation Robots:** Programmed to deliver normalized gait trajectories phase by phase.
- **Orthotic Devices:** Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) are tuned to control specific sub-phases (e.g., restrict plantarflexion at heel strike).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Gait Cycle Percentage vs. Time Conversion

**Problem Setup:** A KTU board question typically provides either cadence (steps/min) or walking speed and asks for the absolute time spent in each phase.

**Given:**
- Cadence $C = 110$ steps/min
- One stride = 2 steps

**Step 1: Convert cadence to cycle frequency**

The number of full gait cycles (strides) per minute is half the cadence:

$$f_{cycle} = \frac{C}{2} = \frac{110}{2} = 55 \text{ cycles/min}$$

**Step 2: Convert to time per cycle**

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{60}{f_{cycle}} = \frac{60}{55} \approx 1.0909 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Compute Stance Phase time**

$$T_{stance} = 0.60 \times T_{cycle} = 0.60 \times 1.0909 \approx 0.6545 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compute Swing Phase time**

$$T_{swing} = 0.40 \times T_{cycle} = 0.40 \times 1.0909 \approx 0.4364 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Compute Double Support time (each period)**

$$T_{DS} = 0.10 \times T_{cycle} = 0.10 \times 1.0909 \approx 0.1091 \text{ s}$$

**Result:** For a 110 steps/min cadence, one full gait cycle lasts ~1.09 s, of which 0.65 s is stance, 0.44 s is swing, and each double-support period is ~0.11 s.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stride Length from Speed and Cadence

**Given:** Walking speed $v = 1.4$ m/s, Cadence $C = 112$ steps/min

**Step 1: Cadence in steps/sec**

$$C_{sec} = \frac{112}{60} \approx 1.8667 \text{ steps/s}$$

**Step 2: Stride frequency (strides per second)**

$$f_{stride} = \frac{C_{sec}}{2} = \frac{1.8667}{2} \approx 0.9333 \text{ strides/s}$$

**Step 3: Stride Length**

$$L_{stride} = \frac{v}{f_{stride}} = \frac{1.4}{0.9333} \approx 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Step Length**

$$L_{step} = \frac{L_{stride}}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Froude Number / Dimensionless Speed

**Why dimensionless speed matters:** Pediatric and adult prosthetics differ enormously in leg length. The Froude number $Fr$ normalizes walking speed against leg length and gravity, allowing phase durations to be compared across body sizes.

**Given:** Walking speed $v = 1.4$ m/s, leg length $L = 0.9$ m, gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Form the Froude number**

$$Fr = \frac{v^{2}}{g \cdot L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$Fr = \frac{(1.4)^{2}}{9.81 \times 0.9} = \frac{1.96}{8.829} \approx 0.222$$

**Step 3: Compare to the walk–run transition threshold**

The walk–run transition occurs at $Fr \approx 0.5$. Since $0.222 < 0.5$, the subject is walking, not running. The Froude number being 0.222 also indicates a normal walking pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to recognize that the ratio of stance-to-swing duration changes systematically with $Fr$. As $Fr$ increases from 0.1 (slow walk) toward 0.5, the stance fraction decreases from ~0.65 to ~0.50, and the gait transitions to running (where stance fraction drops below 0.5 and double support is replaced by a flight phase).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Phase-by-Phase Joint Kinematics

> The following table consolidates joint angle data used by the KTU board for descriptive questions on gait phases. Values are approximate, normal adult, walking speed 1.3 m/s.

| Sub-Phase | % Cycle | Hip Angle (°) | Knee Angle (°) | Ankle Angle (°) | Key Muscle Group Active |
|-----------|---------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Heel Strike | 0 | 20 flexion | 0 (extended) | 0 (neutral) | Hamstrings (eccentric) |
| Foot Flat | 0–12 | 20 flexion | 15 flexion | 5 plantarflexion | Quadriceps (eccentric) |
| Mid-Stance | 12–31 | 5 flexion | 5 flexion | 5 dorsiflexion | Gastrocnemius (eccentric) |
| Heel Off | 31–50 | 10 extension | 0 (extended) | 15 plantarflexion | Gastrocsoleus (concentric) |
| Toe Off | 50–62 | 10 extension | 35 flexion | 20 plantarflexion | Iliopsoas, rectus femoris |
| Acceleration | 62–75 | 15 flexion | 60 flexion | 5 dorsiflexion | Iliopsoas, hip flexors |
| Mid-Swing | 75–87 | 20 flexion | 60 flexion | 0 (neutral) | Tibialis anterior |
| Deceleration | 87–100 | 25 flexion | 5 flexion | 0 (neutral) | Hamstrings (eccentric) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention used:** Flexion is positive. Plantarflexion is positive (toes pointing down). Extension and dorsiflexion are also shown with their respective signs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: The 8 Phases of the Gait Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Heel Strike 0 percent] --> B[Foot Flat 0 to 12 percent]
    B --> C[Mid Stance 12 to 31 percent]
    C --> D[Heel Off 31 to 50 percent]
    D --> E[Toe Off 50 to 62 percent]
    E --> F[Acceleration 62 to 75 percent]
    F --> G[Mid Swing 75 to 87 percent]
    G --> H[Deceleration 87 to 100 percent]
    H --> I[End: Heel Strike 100 percent equals 0 percent of next cycle]
    E -.->|End of Stance| J[STANCE PHASE 60 percent]
    A -.->|Start of Stance| J
    F -.->|Start of Swing| K[SWING PHASE 40 percent]
    H -.->|End of Swing| K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Prosthetic Component Mapping to Gait Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stance Phase Components]
        P1[Heel Component: Simulates Heel Strike Transient]
        P2[Mid Foot Keel: Stores Energy at Foot Flat]
        P3[Forefoot Lever: Push Off at Heel Off and Toe Off]
        P4[Socket and Pylon: Weight Bearing Stability]
    end
    subgraph S2[Swing Phase Components]
        P5[Swing Control Knee Unit]
        P6[Suspension Sleeve or Suction Valve]
        P7[Lightweight Carbon Pylon]
    end
    A1[Phase 1 Heel Strike] --> P1
    A2[Phase 2 Foot Flat] --> P2
    A3[Phase 3 Mid Stance] --> P4
    A4[Phase 4 Heel Off] --> P3
    A5[Phase 5 Toe Off] --> P3
    A6[Phase 6 Acceleration] --> P5
    A7[Phase 7 Mid Swing] --> P7
    A8[Phase 8 Deceleration] --> P5
    A1 --> P6
    A8 --> P6
```

### 4.3 ASCII Stick-Figure Schematic: Position of Limbs at Each Phase

```
   Phase 1 (Heel Strike)        Phase 2 (Foot Flat)
      [body]                       [body]
        |                            |
       /|\                          /|\
        |  (leading leg              |____ (loading
       / \   straight)              / \   on flat foot)

   Phase 3 (Mid-Stance)         Phase 4 (Heel Off)
      [body]                       [body]
        |                            |
       /|\                          /|\
        |  (tibia over              |____ (heel rising,
       / \   foot)                 / \   toe still on)

   Phase 5 (Toe Off)            Phase 6 (Acceleration)
      [body]                       [body]
        |                            |
       /|\                          /|\
        |____ (toe just            /  |  (leg swings
       / \   leaves)              / \   forward)

   Phase 7 (Mid-Swing)          Phase 8 (Deceleration)
      [body]                       [body]
        |                            |
       /|\                          /|\
       /  |  (swing limb           /  |  (leg extending
      / \   under body)           / \   to prepare strike)
```

### 4.4 Stance-vs-Swing Composition (Visual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GC[Gait Cycle 100 percent] --> SP[Stance Phase 60 percent]
    GC --> SWP[Swing Phase 40 percent]
    SP --> DS1[Double Support 1: 0 to 10 percent]
    SP --> SS[Single Support: 10 to 50 percent]
    SP --> DS2[Double Support 2: 50 to 60 percent]
    SWP --> IS[Initial Swing: 60 to 75 percent]
    SWP --> MSW[Mid Swing: 75 to 87 percent]
    SWP --> TSW[Terminal Swing: 87 to 100 percent]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the gait cycle. List the eight phases of the gait cycle and state the percentage duration of the stance and swing phases.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The **gait cycle** is the time interval between two successive occurrences of the same event during walking — conventionally, from one heel strike of a reference foot to the next heel strike of the same foot.
- **(1 mark)** The **eight phases** are: (1) Heel Strike / Initial Contact, (2) Foot Flat / Loading Response, (3) Mid-Stance, (4) Heel Off / Terminal Stance, (5) Toe Off / Pre-Swing, (6) Acceleration / Initial Swing, (7) Mid-Swing, (8) Deceleration / Terminal Swing.
- **(1 mark)** **Stance Phase = 60%** of the cycle, **Swing Phase = 40%** of the cycle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is meant by 'double support period' in gait? How does it change as walking speed increases?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The **double support period** is the phase of the gait cycle during which **both feet are simultaneously in contact with the ground**. There are two such periods in each cycle, each lasting approximately **10% of the cycle** at normal walking speed, totaling 20%.
- **(1 mark)** As walking speed increases, the **double support period decreases**. At very slow speeds it can occupy up to 25–30% of the cycle.
- **(1 mark)** At the **walk-to-run transition** (Fr ≈ 0.5), double support disappears entirely and is replaced by a **double float (flight) phase**, in which neither foot touches the ground.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Analytical / Numerical Type*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A subject walks with a cadence of 120 steps/min. Calculate:
- (i) The duration of one complete gait cycle.
- (ii) The absolute time spent in the stance phase and swing phase.
- (iii) The duration of each double-support period.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **sub-phases of the Stance Phase**, listing the key joint angles at the hip, knee, and ankle for each sub-phase, and explain why the stance phase is biomechanically divided into these five sub-phases.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- **(i) Gait cycle time** (2 marks):

$$C = 120 \text{ steps/min} \implies f_{cycle} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ cycles/min}$$

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{60}{60} = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

- **(ii) Stance and Swing times** (3 marks):

$$T_{stance} = 0.60 \times 1.0 = 0.60 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{swing} = 0.40 \times 1.0 = 0.40 \text{ s}$$

- **(iii) Double support time per period** (2 marks):

$$T_{DS} = 0.10 \times 1.0 = 0.10 \text{ s}$$

**Part (b):**
- **(3 marks for sketch** — a stick figure showing each of the five sub-phases in sequence with body markers at hip, knee, ankle).
- **(3 marks for joint angle table**:

| Sub-Phase | Hip (°) | Knee (°) | Ankle (°) |
|-----------|---------|----------|-----------|
| Heel Strike | 20 flex | 0 | 0 |
| Foot Flat | 20 flex | 15 flex | 5 PF |
| Mid-Stance | 5 flex | 5 flex | 5 DF |
| Heel Off | 10 ext | 0 | 15 PF |
| Toe Off | 10 ext | 35 flex | 20 PF |

- **(1 mark)** Justification: The stance phase is divided because the body transitions through distinct mechanical events — **impact absorption (foot flat), single-limb support (mid-stance), forward propulsion (heel off, toe off)** — each requiring different muscle activation patterns and prosthetic responses.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Descriptive / Conceptual Type*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **three sub-phases of the Swing Phase** of the gait cycle. For each sub-phase, describe the joint positions, dominant muscle actions, and the implications for prosthetic limb design.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the effect of **walking speed** on the relative durations of stance and swing phases. Use the Froude number concept to justify your answer, and explain the significance of the walk-to-run transition in prosthetic prescription.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Swing Phase Sub-phases (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks) Acceleration / Initial Swing (62–75%):** Begins at toe-off. The hip flexor muscles (iliopsoas) actively contract to accelerate the limb forward. The knee rapidly flexes to ~60° to lift the foot off the ground. **Prosthetic implication:** Lightweight components and proper socket suspension are essential; a heavy prosthetic leg will cause excessive swing initiation effort and a "hiking" gait.

- **(2 marks) Mid-Swing (75–87%):** The reference limb passes directly under the body's center of mass. Knee reaches maximum flexion (~60–70°), ankle is in neutral. **Prosthetic implication:** Minimum toe clearance of **1–2 cm** must be maintained to prevent stumbling. Hydraulic/pneumatic swing-control knee units ensure this clearance at varied cadences.

- **(2 marks) Deceleration / Terminal Swing (87–100%):** The limb decelerates in preparation for the next heel strike. The hamstrings contract eccentrically to slow the shank. Knee extends toward neutral. **Prosthetic implication:** The prosthetic knee must have appropriate dampening; an undamped knee will slam into full extension, causing an audible "knee knock" and unsafe gait.

- **(1 mark)** Concluding remark: All three sub-phases are critical for safety, symmetry, and energy efficiency of the amputee's gait.

**Part (b) – Speed and Froude Number (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** As walking speed increases, the **stance phase duration decreases** (from ~62% at very slow walking to ~50% at fast walking) while the **swing phase duration increases**.
- **(2 marks)** The Froude number $Fr = \frac{v^{2}}{gL}$ normalizes speed against leg length and gravity. The walk-run transition occurs at $Fr \approx 0.5$.
- **(2 marks)** At this transition, double support disappears and is replaced by a flight phase. Above this, the gait is no longer "walking" but "running."
- **(1 mark)** **Prosthetic significance:** Prosthetic components are often classified by activity level (K-levels, K0–K4). Higher K-levels demand feet and knees that can handle faster cadences, higher energy return, and shorter stance fractions. A low-activity (K1) patient needs a SACH foot, while a high-activity (K4) patient requires an energy-storing carbon-fiber foot and a microprocessor-controlled knee.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Gait Phase Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing step and stride:** Step = one foot to the other; Stride = same foot to same foot. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Stating "Stance = 62% and Swing = 38%"** as fixed values — KTU expects the student to specify that these are *normal walking* values, and to note they vary with speed. Markers want the **60/40** ratio and a statement on variability.
> 3. **Naming only "Heel Strike" and "Toe Off"** as the two phases — this is the most common mistake. The full answer requires **eight sub-phases**.
> 4. **Ignoring units in numerical answers** — always state the unit (seconds, meters, steps/min).
> 5. **Writing "toe off" instead of "pre-swing"** — Perry's terminology (used in KTU syllabus) is **Pre-Swing**, not Toe Off (toe off is the *event* marking the start of pre-swing).
> 6. **Forgetting double support periods** — when asked for a timeline, students frequently omit the two double-support windows.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Gait Cycle Definition:** One heel strike to the next heel strike of the *same* foot (100%).
- **Two main phases:** Stance (60%) and Swing (40%).
- **Five stance sub-phases:** Heel Strike, Foot Flat, Mid-Stance, Heel Off, Toe Off (Pre-Swing).
- **Three swing sub-phases:** Acceleration (Initial Swing), Mid-Swing, Deceleration (Terminal Swing).
- **Two double-support periods:** Each ~10% of cycle, separated by single support (~40%).
- **Cadence:** 110–120 steps/min normal; one stride = 2 steps.
- **Walking speed:** 1.3–1.5 m/s normal; $v = L_{stride} \times f_{stride}$.
- **Froude Number:** $Fr = v^{2}/(gL)$; walk–run transition at $Fr \approx 0.5$.
- **Duty Factor:** $DF = T_{stance}/T_{cycle}$; walk > 0.5, run < 0.5.
- **Prosthetic mapping:** Heel strike → heel component; Mid-stance → socket stability; Heel off → forefoot lever; Swing → suspension + lightweight pylon.
- **Key muscles:** Quadriceps (loading response), Gastrocsoleus (terminal stance), Iliopsoas (initial swing), Hamstrings (terminal swing).
- **Key joint angles to memorize:** At heel strike — hip 20° flex, knee 0°, ankle 0°. At toe off — hip 10° ext, knee 35° flex, ankle 20° PF.
- **Sign convention:** Flexion and plantarflexion are positive in KTU textbooks.
- **Examiner's mantra:** "Eight phases, 60/40 split, two double-supports, and one stride = two steps."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gait Analysis Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT601 - BIOMECHANICS & DESIGN OF PROSTHETIC DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gait Analysis Methods

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gait Analysis** is the systematic study of human locomotion, involving the measurement, interpretation, and quantification of the biomechanical parameters that characterize walking (and running). It is defined by the **American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS)** and **Gait and Clinical Movement Analysis Society (GCMAS)** as a multi-disciplinary evaluation of human motion that integrates **kinematics** (motion without considering forces), **kinetics** (forces causing motion), and **electromyography (EMG)** (muscle activation patterns) to assess functional walking ability.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, gait analysis methods are classified into two principal families:

| Classification | Definition | Example Techniques |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative (Observational)** | Subjective visual assessment by a trained clinician | Visual Gait Assessment, Video Taping, GMFM Scale |
| **Quantitative (Instrumented)** | Objective, data-driven measurement using sensors | 3D Motion Capture, Force Platforms, Instrumented Walkways, EMG |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to distinguish between **observational (qualitative)** and **instrumented (quantitative)** methods and to describe at least three modern instrumental techniques (motion capture, force plate, EMG).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a train passing through a station. You can either **just look at it** and comment "the train is moving fast" *(observational gait analysis — cheap, quick, but subjective and error-prone)*, **OR** you can place a **speedometer on the tracks**, a **high-speed camera**, and **pressure sensors under the rails** to measure exactly how fast it moves, how much it weighs at each wheel, and how the tracks deform under load *(instrumented gait analysis — expensive, precise, objective, reproducible)*.

Gait analysis works the same way:
- **Observational analysis** = the "naked eye" of a physiotherapist watching a patient walk.
- **Instrumented analysis** = a biomechanics lab where reflective markers, force plates, and EMG electrodes act as the "instruments" measuring every parameter of the walk.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics in Human Gait

The following are universally accepted normative values used clinically (Perry & Burnfield, *Gait Analysis: Normal and Pathological Function*, 2010):

- **Walking Speed:** **1.30 – 1.50 m/s** in healthy adults.
- **Cadence:** **110 – 120 steps/minute**.
- **Stride Length:** **1.30 – 1.50 m** (≈ height × 0.78).
- **Step Length:** **0.65 – 0.75 m** (≈ stride length / 2).
- **Stance Phase:** **60%** of the gait cycle.
- **Swing Phase:** **40%** of the gait cycle.
- **Single Limb Support:** **40%** of the gait cycle.
- **Double Limb Support:** **20%** of the gait cycle (10% initial + 10% terminal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Gait Cycle:** One complete sequence of lower-limb motion from initial heel contact of one foot to the next initial heel contact of the **same** foot. It is the fundamental repeating unit of human walking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Gait Analysis Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a structured understanding of the **five principal methods** of gait analysis. Each method provides a different biomechanical "lens" on the same walking event.

### Method 1 — Observational Gait Analysis (OGA)

- **Principle:** A trained examiner visually inspects the patient walking, often recorded on a video camera, and rates deviations from normal using standardized scales (e.g., **Rancho Los Amigos Observational Gait Analysis Form**, **Edinburgh Visual Gait Score**).
- **Equipment:** Standard video camera (typically at 30–60 fps), marked walkway, stopwatch.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, no instrumentation, useful for rapid clinical screening.
- **Limitations:** Subjective, inter-rater reliability is moderate, cannot quantify joint moments or muscle activity.
- **Why it matters:** Forms the **first line of clinical assessment** before more expensive tests are ordered.

### Method 2 — Video-Based 2D Motion Analysis

- **Principle:** Reflective markers placed on anatomical landmarks are tracked by 2–4 cameras. Software reconstructs marker trajectories in the **sagittal, coronal, and transverse planes**.
- **Equipment:** 2D motion analysis software (e.g., *Kinovea*, *Dartfish*), calibrated camera setup.
- **Advantages:** Inexpensive compared to 3D, suitable for sagittal-plane gait kinematics.
- **Limitations:** Skin marker artefact (~2–3 cm error), 2D projection loses depth information, cannot measure out-of-plane motion accurately.

### Method 3 — Three-Dimensional (3D) Motion Capture (The Gold Standard)

- **Principle:** Multiple infrared cameras (typically 6–12) emit IR light; retro-reflective markers attached to skin or rigid clusters reflect this light. Cameras detect marker positions at high frame rates (100–500 Hz). Triangulation in 3D space reconstructs body segment trajectories.
- **Two Sub-Systems:**
  - **Passive Optical (Marker-Based):** Vicon, Qualisys, Motion Analysis Corporation.
  - **Active Optical:** Markers emit LED pulses (e.g., *Optotrak*, *Polaris*).
- **Output:** Joint angles, joint angular velocities, segment accelerations, full-body skeletal model.
- **Why it matters:** This is the **gold standard for kinematic gait analysis** in KTU 2024 outcomes CO1 and CO2.

### Method 4 — Kinetic (Force Plate) Analysis

- **Principle:** Force platforms embedded in a walkway measure the **three components of the Ground Reaction Force (GRF)** — vertical $\left(F_z\right)$, anterior-posterior $\left(F_y\right)$, and medial-lateral $\left(F_x\right)$ — plus the **Centre of Pressure (CoP)** and **free moment** at high sampling rates (typically **1000 Hz**).
- **Output:** GRF vector, CoP trajectory, joint moments via **Inverse Dynamics**, joint reaction forces, mechanical power.
- **Why it matters:** Force plate data is essential for understanding **loading patterns** during gait, crucial for prosthetic socket design and orthotic prescriptions.

### Method 5 — Electromyography (EMG)

- **Principle:** Surface or intramuscular electrodes detect the electrical potential generated by muscle fibers during contraction. EMG reveals **when** a muscle is activated, **how strongly**, and **for how long**.
- **Two Types:**
  - **Surface EMG (sEMG):** Non-invasive skin electrodes, used for superficial muscles (e.g., tibialis anterior, gastrocnemius).
  - **Fine-Wire / Indwelling EMG:** Needle electrodes for deep muscles (e.g., tibialis posterior).
- **Why it matters:** EMG links the *kinematic* (motion) and *kinetic* (force) data to the **neural control strategy** — explaining *why* a motion or force pattern occurs.

### Supplementary Methods (Often Examined in KTU)

- **Instrumented Walkways (e.g., GAITRite®):** Pressure-sensitive mats that compute spatiotemporal parameters (step length, cadence, velocity) automatically.
- **Plantar Pressure Measurement (e.g., Tekscan F-Scan, Novel Pedar):** Insoles with capacitive sensors measuring in-shoe pressure distribution — critical for diabetic foot and prosthetic alignment.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs):** Body-worn accelerometers and gyroscopes enabling **out-of-lab** gait analysis.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This cheat-sheet covers every formula required for numerical problems on Gait Analysis Methods under KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize the units as carefully as the symbols.

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Walking Speed | $v = \dfrac{L_{stride} \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60}$ | m/s | $L_{stride}$ in m, $n$ = cadence in steps/min |
| 2 | Walking Speed (alternative) | $v = L_{stride} \cdot f_{stride}$ | m/s | $f_{stride}$ = stride frequency in strides/s |
| 3 | Cadence | $n = \dfrac{2v}{L_{stride}} \cdot 60$ | steps/min | Derived from speed and stride length |
| 4 | Dimensionless Walking Speed | $\hat{v} = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{leg}}}$ | — | Used for inter-subject scaling (Froude-like) |
| 5 | Stride Duration | $T_{stride} = \dfrac{60}{f_{stride}}$ | s | Time for one full gait cycle |
| 6 | Vertical GRF peak (walking) | $F_z^{peak} \approx 1.2 \, m g$ | N | Approximately **120% body weight** |
| 7 | Vertical GRF peak (running) | $F_z^{peak} \approx 2.5 \, m g$ | N | Approximately **2.5× body weight** |
| 8 | Newton's Second Law (joint dynamics) | $\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$ | N / (m/s²) | Foundation of inverse dynamics |
| 9 | Joint Moment (Inverse Dynamics) | $M = I \alpha + \sum (\vec{r} \times \vec{F})$ | N·m | $I$ = moment of inertia, $\alpha$ = angular acceleration |
| 10 | Mechanical Power at a Joint | $P = M \cdot \omega$ | W | $M$ = joint moment, $\omega$ = angular velocity |
| 11 | Cadence–Speed Linear Relation | $n = a \cdot v + b$ | steps/min | Empirically $a \approx 75$, $b \approx 60$ for adults |
| 12 | Step Width | $W_{step} \approx 0.08 \, m$ to $0.12 \, m$ | m | Medial-lateral distance between heel centers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When the problem states "cadence = 110 steps/min", remember this counts each footfall, **not** full strides. Therefore the speed formula uses a factor of 2 in the denominator.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters

- **Prosthetics:** Socket alignment of a transtibial prosthesis is optimized using GRF data so that the line of action passes through the knee center during stance.
- **Orthotics:** Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) are designed to control the plantarflexion–dorsiflexion moment based on inverse-dynamics outputs.
- **Rehabilitation:** Stroke patients' gait symmetry is quantified using the **Symmetry Index** (SI).
- **Sports Engineering:** Sprinters' GRF profiles guide track-spike and shoe design.
- **Robotics:** Human gait data informs the control algorithms of bipedal exoskeletons and humanoid robots (e.g., Tesla Optimus, ASIMO).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are exhaustive. **No step is skipped.** Each mathematical transition is followed by a plain-English explanation of *why* we are performing that operation.

## 3.1 Derivation of Walking Speed from Stride Length and Cadence

**Given:**
- Stride length, $L_s = 1.40 \, m$
- Cadence, $n = 112 \, \text{steps/min}$

**Find:** Mean walking speed $v$ in m/s.

---

**Step 1 — Define the variables precisely.**

We define *cadence* $n$ as the number of footsteps per minute, where **each step corresponds to one heel strike**. A complete *stride* consists of **two steps** (one left heel strike + one right heel strike). Therefore the stride frequency $f_s$ in strides per second is half the cadence in steps per second:

$$f_s = \dfrac{n / 60}{2} = \dfrac{n}{120}$$

**Explanation:** Cadence $n$ is given per minute, so we divide by 60 to convert to steps per second. Then we divide by 2 because two steps make one stride.

---

**Step 2 — Express mean walking speed.**

The mean walking speed equals the distance covered per stride multiplied by the number of strides per second:

$$v = L_s \cdot f_s$$

**Explanation:** Speed (m/s) = distance (m) × frequency (1/s). The fundamental kinematic identity.

---

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $f_s$.**

$$v = L_s \cdot \dfrac{n}{120}$$

**Explanation:** This single equation is the universal speed–stride–cadence relation used in every clinical gait lab.

---

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$v = 1.40 \cdot \dfrac{112}{120}$$

**Explanation:** We directly substitute the measured values from the problem.

---

**Step 5 — Compute the arithmetic.**

$$v = 1.40 \cdot 0.9333\ldots = 1.3067 \, m/s$$

**Explanation:** $\dfrac{112}{120} = 0.9333$ strides per second, and multiplying by the stride length gives the speed.

---

**Step 6 — Round to clinically appropriate precision.**

$$\boxed{v \approx 1.31 \, m/s}$$

**Explanation:** Walking speeds in clinical reports are usually quoted to two decimal places. This value falls within the normal adult range of **1.30–1.50 m/s**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Peak Joint Moment using Inverse Dynamics

**Problem Setup:** During mid-stance, the GRF acts at the centre of pressure on the foot. We wish to calculate the **ankle dorsiflexion moment** required to maintain quasi-static equilibrium.

**Given:**
- Vertical GRF at mid-stance, $F_z = 750 \, N$
- Horizontal lever arm from ankle joint centre to CoP, $r = 0.12 \, m$
- Body segment mass below ankle, $m = 4.5 \, kg$
- Sagittal-plane moment of inertia of foot about ankle, $I = 0.045 \, kg \cdot m^2$
- Angular acceleration of foot (assumed quasi-static), $\alpha \approx 0 \, rad/s^2$

**Find:** Ankle joint moment $M_{ankle}$.

---

**Step 1 — State Newton's Second Law for Rotation.**

For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, the sum of external moments equals the moment of inertia times angular acceleration:

$$\sum M = I \cdot \alpha$$

**Explanation:** This is the rotational form of $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$. The ankle is treated as a pivot for the foot segment.

---

**Step 2 — Identify the moments acting on the foot.**

Two moments act on the foot at mid-stance:
- **External moment** from the GRF: $M_{ext} = F_z \cdot r$
- **Internal muscle/tendon moment** generated by the ankle plantarflexors (gastrocnemius–soleus): $M_{ankle}$

The equilibrium equation is:

$$M_{ankle} - F_z \cdot r = I \cdot \alpha$$

**Explanation:** The internal muscle moment must balance the external GRF moment; any imbalance results in angular acceleration of the foot.

---

**Step 3 — Substitute $I \cdot \alpha = 0$ (quasi-static assumption).**

$$M_{ankle} - F_z \cdot r = 0$$

**Explanation:** Mid-stance is approximately quasi-static — the foot is neither accelerating nor decelerating rapidly, so angular acceleration is taken as zero.

---

**Step 4 — Solve for $M_{ankle}$.**

$$M_{ankle} = F_z \cdot r$$

**Explanation:** This is the classic inverse-dynamics result. **The joint moment equals the external force times the perpendicular lever arm.**

---

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$M_{ankle} = 750 \cdot 0.12$$

**Explanation:** 750 N (≈ 76 kg) acting through a 12 cm lever.

---

**Step 6 — Compute.**

$$M_{ankle} = 90 \, N \cdot m$$

**Explanation:** A 90 N·m ankle moment at mid-stance is typical of normal walking; it is generated primarily by the triceps surae (gastrocnemius and soleus).

---

**Step 7 — Direction convention.**

Since the GRF vector passes **anterior** to the ankle joint, it creates a **plantarflexion** moment. The ankle plantarflexors must therefore generate an **equal and opposite dorsiflexion-resisting moment**. By convention, this is reported as a **plantarflexor moment**:

$$\boxed{M_{ankle} = 90 \, N \cdot m \quad (\text{plantarflexor, internal})}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Symmetry Index Calculation

The **Symmetry Index (SI)** is a KTU-favourite metric for quantifying left–right asymmetry in pathological gait:

$$SI(\%) = \dfrac{\vert X_{affected} - X_{unaffected} \vert}{0.5 \cdot (X_{affected} + X_{unaffected})} \cdot 100$$

**Given:**
- Step length of affected limb, $L_{aff} = 0.52 \, m$
- Step length of unaffected limb, $L_{unaff} = 0.74 \, m$

**Find:** SI in %.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator.**

$$\vert 0.52 - 0.74 \vert = 0.22$$

**Explanation:** Absolute difference between the two step lengths.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator.**

$$0.5 \cdot (0.52 + 0.74) = 0.5 \cdot 1.26 = 0.63$$

**Explanation:** This is the mean of the two step lengths — used to "normalize" the SI so it can be compared across patients of different sizes.

---

**Step 3 — Form the ratio and convert to percent.**

$$SI = \dfrac{0.22}{0.63} \cdot 100$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by the mean and multiplying by 100 expresses the asymmetry as a percentage.

---

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$SI = 34.92\%$$

**Explanation:** A value above 10% is generally considered clinically significant asymmetry.

$$\boxed{SI \approx 34.92\% \quad (\text{severely asymmetric})}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Clinical Question | Best Gait Analysis Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Quick screening of stroke gait deviation | Observational / Video | Fast, no setup, low cost |
| Precise joint angle waveform | 3D Motion Capture | Sub-degree accuracy, full 3D |
| Loading during stance | Force Plate | Only direct measurement of GRF |
| Muscle activation timing | EMG | Only method revealing neural control |
| Diabetic foot ulcer risk | Plantar Pressure Insoles | Maps in-shoe pressure hotspots |
| Long-term home monitoring | IMUs | Wearable, captures real-life gait |
| Spatiotemporal parameters only | Instrumented Walkway (GAITRite) | Automatic, validated, fast |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Gait Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Walks on Instrumented Walkway] --> B[Data Acquisition Module]
    A1[Video Cameras 30 to 60 fps] --> B
    A2[Optical Motion Capture 100 to 500 Hz] --> B
    A3[Force Platforms 1000 Hz] --> B
    A4[EMG Electrodes 1000 Hz] --> B
    B --> C[Signal Processing and Filtering]
    C --> D[Biomechanical Model Reconstruction]
    D --> E[Kinematic Analysis]
    D --> F[Kinetic Analysis]
    D --> G[EMG Envelope Analysis]
    E --> H[Joint Angles and Angular Velocities]
    F --> I[Ground Reaction Forces and Joint Moments]
    G --> J[Muscle Activation Timings]
    H --> K[Clinical Report Generation]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Diagnosis and Treatment Planning]
    K --> M[Prosthetic and Orthotic Design Input]
    K --> N[Rehabilitation Progress Tracking]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Tree for Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Clinical Gait Assessment Required] --> Q{Budget Available?}
    Q -- No --> R[Observational Gait Analysis]
    Q -- Yes --> S{3D Motion Needed?}
    R --> R1[Use Rancho Los Amigos or Edinburgh Visual Gait Score]
    S -- Yes --> T[3D Optical Motion Capture System]
    S -- No --> U[Instrumented Walkway GAITRite]
    T --> V{Force Data Needed?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Add Force Platforms and EMG]
    V -- No --> X[Kinematics Only Report]
    U --> Y{Plantar Pressure Needed?}
    Y -- Yes --> Z[Add Tekscan F-Scan or Novel Pedar Insoles]
    Y -- No --> AA[Spatiotemporal Parameter Report]
    W --> AB[Full Inverse Dynamics and EMG Report]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Synchronization of Multi-Modal Data

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Patient
    participant MC as Motion Capture
    participant FP as Force Plate
    participant EMG as EMG System
    participant SW as Analysis Software
    P->>MC: Walks past reflective markers
    MC->>SW: Marker trajectories 200 Hz
    P->>FP: Heel strike on force plate
    FP->>SW: GRF components 1000 Hz
    P->>EMG: Muscle activation during gait
    EMG->>SW: Raw EMG signals 1000 Hz
    SW->>SW: Time-sync all streams via hardware trigger
    SW->>SW: Compute joint angles and moments
    SW-->>Clinician: Unified gait report PDF
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inverse Dynamics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Marker Trajectories] --> B[Segment Pose Estimation]
    C[GRF from Force Plate] --> B
    B --> D[Segment Velocities and Accelerations]
    D --> E[Newton Euler Equations per Segment]
    E --> F[Distal to Proximal Joint Reaction Forces]
    F --> G[Joint Moments]
    G --> H[Muscle and Tendon Force Estimation]
    G --> I[Mechanical Power P equals M times omega]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**Define gait analysis. List any two instrumented methods of gait analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Gait analysis is the systematic study of human locomotion involving the measurement and evaluation of kinematic, kinetic, and EMG parameters during walking, to identify normal and pathological movement patterns.

**Two instrumented methods (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Three-dimensional motion capture system** — uses infrared cameras and reflective markers to track body segment trajectories and compute joint kinematics.
2. **Force plate (Kinetic analysis)** — measures the three components of ground reaction force (vertical, anterior-posterior, medial-lateral) and the centre of pressure during stance phase.

*(Alternative valid methods accepted: EMG, instrumented walkway GAITRite, plantar pressure insoles, IMUs.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**What is cadence? State its normal range in healthy adults.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cadence (2 marks):** Cadence is defined as the number of steps (footfalls) taken per unit time, conventionally expressed in **steps per minute**. Each step corresponds to one heel strike event.

**Normal range (1 mark):** The normal cadence in healthy adults is **110 – 120 steps/minute**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module-1 Part-B question carries an internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** Describe the **observational method** of gait analysis. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **3D optical motion capture system** for gait analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — Observational Gait Analysis

**1. Introduction (2 marks):**
Observational Gait Analysis (OGA) is the **qualitative, visual assessment** of a patient's walking pattern by a trained clinician. It is the oldest and most widely used form of gait analysis, requiring no electronic instrumentation beyond a video camera and a stopwatch.

**2. Procedure (3 marks):**
- The patient walks a marked walkway (typically 8–10 m long) at a self-selected speed.
- The clinician observes from **three views** — anterior, posterior, and lateral (sagittal).
- Movements are recorded on video (30–60 fps) for slow-motion playback.
- Standardized rating forms are completed — e.g., the **Rancho Los Amigos Observational Gait Analysis Form** (which itemizes deviations at the pelvis, hip, knee, ankle, and foot during stance and swing).
- Spatiotemporal parameters (step length, cadence, speed) are measured using a **stopwatch and footprint markers** left on a paper walkway (the **ink-footprint method**).

**3. Advantages (1 mark):**
- Low cost, no calibration needed, rapid screening, suitable for bedside assessment.

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Highly subjective; inter-rater reliability is moderate; cannot measure joint moments or muscle activity; visual occlusion of body segments.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 3D Optical Motion Capture System

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

(See Mermaid Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**2. Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Multiple **infrared (IR) cameras** (typically 6–12) surround a calibrated capture volume.
- Each camera contains an **IR LED ring** that floods the volume with infrared light.
- **Retro-reflective spherical markers** (12–16 mm diameter) placed on anatomical landmarks (e.g., ASIS, PSIS, greater trochanter, lateral femoral epicondyle, lateral malleolus, calcaneus, second metatarsal head) reflect the IR light back to the cameras.
- A **lens and photosensor** in each camera detects the 2D image of each marker.
- A central workstation uses **direct linear transformation (DLT)** or **epipolar geometry** to triangulate the 2D images into 3D coordinates at sampling rates of **100–500 Hz**.

**3. Data Processing (1 mark):**
- Marker trajectories are filtered (e.g., 4th-order Butterworth low-pass filter, 6 Hz cutoff).
- A **linked-segment biomechanical model** (e.g., Helen Hayes, Plug-in Gait) reconstructs joint centres and computes joint angles.

**4. Output Parameters (1 mark):**
- 3D joint angles, angular velocities, angular accelerations; spatiotemporal parameters; segment trajectories — used by clinicians for diagnosis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a force platform** used in gait analysis. Sketch a typical vertical GRF curve during normal walking and label its characteristic peaks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **electromyography (EMG)**. Distinguish between surface EMG and indwelling EMG. List any two clinical applications of EMG in gait analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — Force Platform

**1. Working Principle (3 marks):**
A force platform is a rigid plate mounted on **piezoelectric** or **strain-gauge load cells** at its corners. When the foot strikes the plate, the vertical, anterior-posterior, and medial-lateral components of the ground reaction force (GRF) produce minute deformations in the load cells. These deformations are converted into proportional electrical voltages via the **Wheatstone bridge** circuit, sampled at **1000 Hz** or higher, and digitally recorded.

**2. Output (1 mark):**
- Three force components: $F_x$ (medial-lateral), $F_y$ (anterior-posterior), $F_z$ (vertical).
- Centre of Pressure (CoP) location: $CoP_x = \dfrac{-M_y}{F_z}$, $CoP_y = \dfrac{M_x}{F_z}$
- Free moment about the vertical axis.

**3. Vertical GRF Curve — Sketch and Peaks (3 marks):**

The vertical GRF trace during normal walking is the classic **"M-shaped" double-hump curve**:

- **First peak (Fz1):** Occurs at **~20% of stance phase**, value ≈ **1.2 × body weight**. Caused by weight acceptance and deceleration of the body's downward momentum.
- **Valley (Fz_min):** Occurs at **~30–40% stance**, value ≈ **0.7–0.8 × BW**. Corresponds to mid-stance single-limb support.
- **Second peak (Fz2):** Occurs at **~50% stance**, value ≈ **1.1–1.2 × BW**. Caused by push-off and forward propulsion.

*(Fz1 and Fz2 are sometimes called the **heel-strike transient** and **push-off peak** respectively. In pathological gait these peaks may be reduced, flattened, or absent.)*

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — EMG

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
**Electromyography (EMG)** is the technique of recording the electrical activity associated with muscle contraction, providing information about *when* and *how strongly* a muscle is activated during movement.

**2. Surface vs Indwelling EMG (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

| Feature | Surface EMG (sEMG) | Indwelling (Needle/Fine-wire) EMG |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Skin-mounted Ag/AgCl electrodes | Bipolar needle or fine-wire inserted into muscle belly |
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive |
| Muscles accessible | Only superficial muscles (e.g., tibialis anterior, gastrocnemius) | Deep muscles (e.g., tibialis posterior, iliopsoas) |
| Signal character | Sum of many motor units; cross-talk possible | Selective single or few motor units |
| Clinical use | Gait labs, sports screening | EMG labs for diagnosis of neuropathy, myopathy |

**3. Clinical Applications in Gait Analysis (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Timing of muscle activation** — Identifies whether the tibialis anterior fires appropriately during heel strike and toe-off in a stroke patient; reveals **delayed onset**, **early shutdown**, or **continuous co-contraction**.
2. **Prosthetic control** — In advanced myoelectric prostheses, EMG signals from residual limb muscles (e.g., biceps, triceps) are decoded to drive motorized hand, wrist, or elbow joints.
3. **Botulinum toxin / spasticity management** — EMG-guided injections in cerebral palsy; pre- and post-injection gait EMG patterns quantify outcome.

*(Other valid applications: gait pattern classification in Parkinson's disease, biofeedback training for drop-foot rehabilitation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Cadence unit confusion:** Many students write "cadence = 110 steps/min" and then use it as *strides/min* in the speed formula, resulting in a doubled answer. Always convert: **2 steps = 1 stride**.
> 2. **Missing direction of joint moments:** When asked "find the ankle moment", simply writing the magnitude (e.g., 90 N·m) without stating **plantarflexion** vs **dorsiflexion** loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to label the axes of the GRF curve:** A vertical GRF sketch without the %-stance axis and the two peaks labelled ($F_{z1}$, $F_{z2}$) loses 2 marks out of 3.
> 4. **Confusing "kinematics" with "kinetics":** Kinematics = motion only (angles, velocities); Kinetics = forces and moments. Examiners routinely test this distinction.
> 5. **EMG confusion:** Many students write "EMG measures muscle force" — it actually measures **electrical activity**, which is *correlated* with force but not equal to it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Essential Concepts:**
> - Gait cycle = one full stride = **2 steps** = **stance (60%) + swing (40%)**.
> - **Stance phase** sub-divisions: Initial Double Support (10%) → Single Limb Support (40%) → Terminal Double Support (10%).
> - **Normal cadence** = 110–120 steps/min; normal speed = 1.30–1.50 m/s; stride length ≈ 1.30–1.50 m.
> - The **walking speed formula** $v = L_s \cdot n / 120$ is the single most-tested equation in this module.
> - The **vertical GRF curve** has **two peaks (~1.2 BW each)** at ~20% and ~50% of stance, separated by a mid-stance valley.
> - **Kinematics** = motion (no forces); **Kinetics** = forces & moments; **EMG** = muscle electrical activity.

> **Methods to Memorize:**
> - **Observational** — qualitative, low-cost, subjective.
> - **3D Motion Capture** — kinematic gold standard; marker-based or markerless.
> - **Force Platform** — only direct measure of GRF and CoP.
> - **EMG** — surface (non-invasive) vs indwelling (invasive).
> - **Instrumented Walkway** — automated spatiotemporal data.
> - **Plantar Pressure Insoles** — in-shoe pressure mapping.
> - **IMUs** — wearable, out-of-lab, real-world gait.

> **Key Equations:**
> - $v = L_s \cdot n / 120$
> - $\sum M = I \alpha$
> - $M = F \cdot r$ (quasi-static)
> - $P = M \cdot \omega$
> - $SI(\%) = \dfrac{\vert X_L - X_R \vert}{0.5(X_L + X_R)} \cdot 100$
> - Peak vertical GRF (walking) ≈ **1.2 BW**; (running) ≈ **2.5 BW**.

> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:**
> - **CO1 (Biomechanics Foundations):** Define gait and gait cycle parameters.
> - **CO2 (Instrumentation):** Describe motion capture, force plate, EMG.
> - **CO3 (Application):** Interpret gait data for prosthetic/orthotic design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT601 - BIOMECHANICS & DESIGN OF PROSTHETIC DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor (KTU Definition):** A *sensor* is a transducer device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus (the *measurand*) into an equivalent electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency) that can be measured, recorded, and analyzed. In the context of biomechanics and prosthetic devices, sensors act as the **sensory interface** between the human body (or its artificial substitute) and the electronic control unit of the instrument.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Distinction:** The terms *sensor* and *transducer* are often used interchangeably, but technically a *transducer* is the broader device (sensor + actuator). A *sensor* is strictly a *passive* input device that only *receives* energy from the system being measured.

The measurable output of a sensor is governed by the general input-output relation:

$$ y(t) = f\big[x(t)\big] + \epsilon(t) $$

where $y(t)$ is the electrical output, $x(t)$ is the input measurand, $f$ is the static transfer function, and $\epsilon(t)$ represents measurement noise and error.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at a Conference"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a person who only speaks **Malayalam** (the measurand — say, knee joint pressure) sitting next to a person who only understands **English** (the electronic circuit reading a voltage). The sensor is the *translator sitting between them*. The Malabari speaker says something, the translator converts it into a voltage, and the English-speaker "understands" it. Without the sensor, the circuit has *no idea* what is happening inside the knee.

In a prosthetic leg:
- The **body / environment** speaks in mechanical language (force, angle, temperature).
- The **microcontroller** only speaks in electrical language (Volts, Amps).
- The **sensor** is the real-time interpreter that makes communication possible.

## 1.3 Why Sensors Are Critical in Prosthetic Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern **bionic prostheses** (smart-knee, myoelectric arm, neural-controlled limb), the sensor is the *first* and most critical link in the signal chain. A poor sensor = a poor prosthetic, regardless of how sophisticated the actuator or controller is.

Key engineering constants / standards referenced in this module:

| Quantity | Standard Value / Symbol |
|----------|--------------------------|
| Room Temperature | **25 °C (298.15 K)** |
| Standard Gravity | **g = 9.80665 m/s²** |
| Body Core Temperature | **37 °C (310.15 K)** |
| Normal Gait Cadence | **~100–120 steps/min** |
| Typical EMG Bandwidth | **20–500 Hz** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Classification of Sensors (with Prosthetic Examples)

Sensors can be classified along multiple axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following taxonomy:

### 2.1.1 Based on the Energy Conversion Mechanism

- **Active Sensors (Self-Generating):** Generate their own electrical output directly from the input energy. *No external excitation required.*
  - *Examples:* Piezoelectric force sensors, thermocouples, photovoltaic cells.
- **Passive Sensors (Externally-Powered):** Modify an external excitation signal in response to the measurand.
  - *Examples:* Strain gauges (resistive), LVDTs (inductive), capacitive pressure sensors.

### 2.1.2 Based on the Measurand

| Measurand Type | Sensor Examples | Prosthetic Application |
|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Force / Pressure | Strain gauge, piezoelectric, FSR | Socket pressure mapping, ground reaction force |
| Displacement / Position | LVDT, encoder, Hall-effect | Joint angle measurement in smart-knee |
| Velocity / Acceleration | Accelerometer, gyroscope | Gait phase detection |
| Bioelectric | EMG, ECG, EEG electrodes | Myoelectric arm control, neural interface |
| Temperature | Thermistor, RTD | Skin-interface monitoring |
| Chemical / Humidity | pH electrode, biosensor | Sweat analysis, wound monitoring |

### 2.1.3 Based on Output Signal Type

- **Analog Sensors** — continuous voltage/current output (e.g., strain gauge bridge).
- **Digital Sensors** — discrete pulse/frequency/coded output (e.g., optical encoders, MEMS IMU with I²C).

## 2.2 Static and Dynamic Characteristics of Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A sensor is only as good as its **static** and **dynamic** characteristics. KTU questions frequently test the *definitions* of these parameters.

### Static Characteristics
- **Accuracy:** Closeness of measurement to the *true value*.
- **Precision (Repeatability):** Closeness of *repeated* measurements to each other.
- **Sensitivity ($S$):** Ratio of change in output to change in input.
- **Linearity:** Deviation of the actual transfer curve from an ideal straight line.
- **Hysteresis:** Maximum difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.
- **Resolution:** Smallest measurable increment of input.
- **Drift:** Slow change in output when input is held constant.
- **Dead Zone / Threshold:** Minimum input required to produce a detectable output.

### Dynamic Characteristics
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** For a first-order sensor, time to reach 63.2% of final value.
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from 10% to 90% of final value.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to settle within a tolerance band (typically 2% or 5%).
- **Bandwidth (BW):** Frequency range over which sensor maintains a flat response.
- **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$) & Damping Ratio ($\zeta$):** Critical for second-order sensors (e.g., accelerometers).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the following table — it represents the *high-yield* equations most commonly asked in KTU ESE Part A and Part B questions on Sensors.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output unit / Input unit |
| 2 | Strain Gauge Sensitivity (Gauge Factor) | $G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\epsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\epsilon}$ | Dimensionless, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 3 | Resistance of a Wire | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_o = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)$ | Balanced when $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ |
| 5 | Piezoelectric Charge | $q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = charge sensitivity (C/N) |
| 6 | Piezoelectric Voltage | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C}$ | $C$ = sensor capacitance |
| 7 | LVDT Output | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $x$ = core displacement |
| 8 | Accelerometer (Mass-Spring-Damper) | $a = \dfrac{k \cdot x}{m} + \ddot{x} + \dfrac{c \cdot \dot{x}}{m}$ | Simplified inertial model |
| 9 | First-Order Step Response | $y(t) = Y_{final}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $\tau$ = time constant |
| 10 | Second-Order Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s |
| 11 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Thermistor (NTC) | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | $\beta$ = material constant (K) |
| 13 | Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = S_{TC} \cdot \Delta T$ | $S_{TC}$ in $\mu$V/°C |
| 14 | Hall-Effect Voltage | $V_H = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$ | Magnetic flux density $B$ sensed |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **myoelectric prosthetic arm**, surface EMG electrodes (sensors) detect the electrical activity of residual muscles. The signal is filtered, rectified, and used to drive a motor in the prosthetic hand. **Without the sensor, no command can reach the prosthesis.** The same is true for **C-Leg (Ottobock)** and **Genium** smart-knees, which use load cells + accelerometers + gyroscopes to detect stair-descent, stumble recovery, and cycling mode.

This is the *Why* of the entire module — sensors are the **eyes, ears, and skin** of any prosthetic device.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gauge Factor of a Resistive Strain Gauge

> [!NOTE]
> A strain gauge is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU exams on sensors. Practice this *word-for-word* for the 7-mark sub-part.

**Starting Point — Resistance of a uniform wire:**

$$ R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A} $$

**Step 1: Take natural logarithm of both sides to enable differentiation.**
